name: LivePerson Inc
description: Pioneer in enterprise conversational AI and chatbot technology, providing the Conversational Cloud platform that enables businesses to automate customer interactions via messaging, voice, and AI-powered bots. Founded in 1995 and once the dominant enterprise chatbot vendor. Revenue peaked at ~$514M in 2022. Executed a 1:15 reverse stock split in Oct 2025.
disruption: Generative AI has commoditized the conversational-AI layer that LivePerson once sold as proprietary technology, making it easier for enterprises and larger cloud/contact-center vendors to build capable bots without LivePerson. Customer cancellations, downsells, and weak new bookings have driven a steep multiyear revenue decline. The decline is not AI-only: disposals, legacy product and execution problems, technology debt, management turnover, and a heavy debt burden are material co-drivers. LivePerson's Bella and other LLM products are a defensive pivot, but they have not yet restored retention or growth. The 1:15 reverse split in October 2025 is handled in the split-adjusted price baseline.

name: Chegg Inc
description: Online education platform offering textbook rentals, homework help, tutoring, and study tools to college and high school students. Peaked at ~$14.7B market cap during COVID remote-learning boom.
disruption: The most direct AI casualty in public markets. ChatGPT and other free AI tools have replaced Chegg's core homework help product, causing subscriber losses of 30%+ YoY. Stock has fallen 99% from its 2021 peak. Non-subscriber traffic dropped 49% after Google AI Overviews launched. Company has cut 45% of workforce and sued Google over AI search summaries.

name: TTEC Holdings Inc
description: Global customer experience (CX) outsourcing firm operating call centers and contact centers across 22 countries with ~50,000 employees. Provides outsourced customer care, technical support, and digital CX consulting.
disruption: Generative-AI chatbots and virtual agents are reducing the routine interaction volumes and human-agent seats that underpin TTEC's contact-center outsourcing and technology revenue. Clients have delayed or resized programs while deciding how much service work to automate, creating a structural risk to seat- and labor-based economics. Recent weakness is mixed rather than AI-only: soft discretionary CX spending, client concentration, restructuring and impairment charges, and elevated leverage also weigh on results. TTEC's AI-enabled services can win transformation work, but automation still lowers the labor volume available to outsource.

name: Endava plc
description: London-headquartered technology services company providing outsourced software development, digital transformation, and IT consulting. Operates nearshore delivery centers across Eastern Europe (Romania, Moldova, Bulgaria) and Latin America, serving clients in financial services, payments, healthcare, and retail. ~11,000 employees.
disruption: AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude) directly reduce the volume of outsourced software development work that is Endava's core business. Clients can accomplish more with fewer external developers, shrinking the billable hours and headcount-based engagements that drive revenue. Stock has fallen ~93% from its Nov 2022 level (~$71 to ~$5). Revenue declined 5-9% YoY through FY2026, and the company swung to a loss as it invested heavily in an AI pivot (Dava.Flow methodology) that has yet to offset the erosion of traditional time-and-materials contracts. Management explicitly warns of near-term revenue headwinds from AI-driven automation compressing legacy delivery models.

name: Getty Images Holdings Inc
description: Global visual content marketplace providing stock photos, editorial images, videos, and music to businesses and media. Library of 700M+ files. Announced $3.7B merger with Shutterstock in Jan 2025.
disruption: AI image generators (Midjourney, DALL-E, Stable Diffusion, Adobe Firefly) directly displace demand for generic stock photography. Getty's Creative segment declined ~5% YoY in 2024. Billions of AI images generated monthly now compete with stock libraries. Getty has tried to pivot by licensing its data to AI companies and building its own AI generator, but the core business of selling individual photo licenses is eroding.

name: Forrester Research Inc
description: Global research and advisory firm providing proprietary analyst reports, data, consulting, and events to technology and business leaders. Sells premium access to research insights, vendor evaluations (Wave reports), and strategic advisory services. Revenue peaked at ~$530M in 2022 before declining to ~$397M in 2025, with a projected further drop to $345-360M in 2026.
disruption: LLMs can synthesize markets, compare vendors, and draft strategic recommendations, weakening the scarcity and pricing power of human-curated analyst research. Forrester's declining contract value, consulting and event pressure, workforce reductions, and decision to exit strategy consulting make that substitution risk material. The operating decline is not attributable to AI alone: the migration to the Forrester Decisions product, weak discretionary research budgets, consulting execution, and event cyclicality are important co-drivers. AI Access is both a defensive product and evidence that the research workflow is moving toward lower-cost, on-demand synthesis.

name: RWS Holdings plc
description: UK-based provider of language services, translation, localization, and intellectual property (patent) services. One of the world's largest translation companies with operations in 100+ languages. Market-leading Trados translation software.
disruption: AI-powered machine translation (DeepL, Google Translate, ChatGPT) has commoditized basic translation work that was RWS's bread-and-butter. Stock down 57% YoY. Adjusted profit fell 43% in FY2025. Took £88M goodwill impairment on Language Services division. Price pressure from clients expecting AI-level efficiency at lower cost. The company is attempting a pivot to 'AI-enabled content solutions' but margins are compressing during the transition.

name: Globant SA
description: Argentina-founded global digital and cognitive transformation company providing AI-driven software development, digital product engineering, and technology consulting. Operates nearshore delivery centers across Latin America (Argentina, Colombia, Brazil, Mexico, Uruguay) and increasingly in India and Eastern Europe. ~29,000 employees serving enterprise clients in financial services, media, technology, and consumer sectors. Revenue peaked at ~$2.45B in 2025. Market cap ~$1.9B, down from ~$8B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: AI coding agents and software-delivery automation reduce the developer hours and team sizes that support Globant's traditional time-and-materials work. The move toward smaller AI-assisted pods and token- or outcome-based pricing acknowledges that labor intensity is falling. Recent revenue deceleration is mixed: cautious client budgets, delayed discretionary programs, customer concentration, and ordinary IT-services cyclicality also matter, while AI projects provide new demand. The thesis is therefore compression of the legacy delivery model, not a claim that every current revenue shortfall was caused by AI.

name: Five9 Inc
description: Cloud contact center software provider offering intelligent virtual agents (IVA), automatic call distribution, IVR, omnichannel routing, workforce management, and analytics for enterprise customer service operations. The leading independent CCaaS (Contact Center as a Service) platform with ~3,000 customers. Revenue peaked at ~$1B run rate in 2023. Stock peaked above $200 in 2021.
disruption: AI voice and service agents threaten Five9's per-human-agent licensing base because successful automation reduces the number of contact-center seats customers need. That is a genuine monetization risk even as Five9 sells its own AI agents and usage-based products. Current evidence does not show an AI-driven revenue collapse: revenue and enterprise AI bookings are still growing, and execution, competition, sales cycles, and contact-center spending also explain the slowdown. The stock reflects a market repricing of the legacy seat model and uncertainty over whether AI usage revenue can offset seat cannibalization.

name: WPP PLC
description: World's largest advertising and marketing services holding company, owning agencies like GroupM, Ogilvy, and AKQA. Provides advertising, media buying, PR, branding, and digital marketing services globally.
disruption: Generative AI can automate portions of creative production, media planning, optimization, and content adaptation, reducing billable agency labor and making it easier for clients and platforms to bring work in-house. WPP's investment in AI is necessary but also shifts value from labor hours toward technology and outcomes. Recent weakness is mixed: major client losses, weak discretionary advertising, execution, restructuring, and geographic exposure are material alongside AI. The qualifying thesis is structural pressure on agency scope and pricing, not that AI alone caused WPP's revenue and share-price decline.

name: Concentrix Corp
description: Global technology-enabled business process outsourcing (BPO) company providing customer experience solutions, digital engineering, and technology services. ~300,000 employees across 40+ countries. Merged with Webhelp in 2023.
disruption: AI chatbots and autonomous service agents reduce routine customer interactions and the human seats that support Concentrix's labor-intensive outsourcing model. Clients are increasingly asking for automation and outcome pricing, which can lower revenue even when Concentrix supplies the technology. The downturn is not AI-only: the Webhelp acquisition, integration costs, leverage, client-specific volume changes, and weak discretionary CX spending are significant co-drivers. AI-enabled transformation work may partly offset lost agent volume, but the legacy FTE model remains structurally exposed.

name: Clarivate PLC
description: Global provider of research analytics, intellectual property intelligence, and scientific data services. Owns Web of Science (academic citation database), Derwent (patent analytics), CompuMark (trademark research), and LifeSci/Healthcare data platforms. Serves researchers, universities, law firms, pharma companies, and governments. Revenue of ~$2.46B in 2025. Market cap ~$1.7B, down from ~$12B at peak.
disruption: Generative AI lowers the cost of searching, summarizing, and comparing scientific, patent, and professional information, putting pressure on Clarivate's premium research and transactional products. Analyst concern about AI and falling transactional revenue support a real structural risk, while Clarivate's Nexus and Claude integrations are a defensive response. The decline is mixed: high leverage, portfolio changes and divestitures, execution, customer budgets, and legacy product complexity also materially affect revenue and valuation. The thesis is weakened pricing power for synthesis and search, not that AI caused the whole contraction.

name: ManpowerGroup Inc
description: Global workforce solutions and staffing company providing temporary and permanent recruitment, talent management, and workforce consulting across 75+ countries. One of the world's largest staffing firms.
disruption: AI automates sourcing, screening, matching, scheduling, and some white-collar tasks that staffing firms monetize, creating a structural risk to placement volume and fees. ManpowerGroup's current revenue weakness, however, is still dominated by the cyclical hiring slowdown, employer caution, and geographic labor-market conditions rather than a quantified AI hit. It remains in the index because AI can make the eventual staffing recovery less labor-intensive and further compress recruiter economics, but the causal story is explicitly mixed.

name: Robert Half Inc
description: Staffing and consulting firm specializing in accounting, finance, technology, legal, and administrative professional placements. Also operates Protiviti consulting subsidiary. Focused on white-collar professional staffing.
disruption: AI threatens Robert Half's white-collar staffing and consulting volumes by automating recruiting workflows and reducing demand for some contract coding, accounting, administrative, and legal tasks. Current results do not isolate AI as the main cause: the post-pandemic staffing correction, a low-hire labor market, client caution, and normal professional-staffing cyclicality remain major drivers. The structural thesis is that AI can cap placement volumes and fees through the recovery, not that all of Robert Half's recent decline was AI-caused.

name: Fiverr International Ltd
description: Online marketplace connecting businesses with freelancers offering digital services including graphic design, copywriting, programming, video editing, and marketing. Platform for gig economy knowledge work.
disruption: AI tools directly replace many of the freelance services sold on Fiverr: AI can now write copy, generate logos, create marketing content, code simple applications, and produce videos. This threatens both the volume of freelance gigs and the pricing power of freelancers. The platform faces compression from both sides—fewer buyers need freelancers for AI-automatable tasks, and freelancers using AI tools face pricing pressure.

name: Shutterstock Inc
description: Global marketplace for licensed stock photographs, vectors, illustrations, videos, and music. Library of 900M+ images. Being acquired by Getty Images in $3.7B deal announced Jan 2025.
disruption: Same dynamic as Getty: AI image generators (DALL-E, Midjourney, Adobe Firefly) directly replace demand for stock photos, especially for generic/commercial use cases. Shutterstock's core download revenue is declining, masked by ~$100M in one-off AI data licensing deals (e.g., $250M OpenAI deal through 2027). Once these licensing deals are normalized, the underlying business erosion becomes visible.

name: Perion Network Ltd
description: Israeli digital advertising technology company specializing in search advertising distribution, display/video advertising, and publisher monetization. Built its business on a lucrative partnership with Microsoft Bing's search distribution marketplace, which at peak contributed ~35% of revenue. Revenue hit $743M in 2023 before collapsing. Market cap ~$364M.
disruption: A casualty of Microsoft's AI pivot in search. When Microsoft integrated Copilot into Bing and restructured its search advertising ecosystem for an AI-first world, it fundamentally changed the search distribution marketplace that Perion depended on. In Q1 2024, Bing implemented ad pricing and mechanism changes that reduced Perion's search advertising activity. Then in June 2024, Microsoft excluded Perion and other publishers from the Bing search distribution marketplace entirely. Search revenue from Bing went from ~35% of total revenue to under 5% in H2 2024. The Bing contract was not renewed at year-end 2024. Stock fell 68% from $26.60 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$8.57. Full-year 2024 revenue dropped 33% and the company swung to a net loss. Perion is pivoting to AI-driven advertising solutions (CTV, retail media, DOOH) but the core search monetization business was destroyed by Microsoft's AI transformation of Bing.

name: Asana Inc
description: Cloud-based work management and project collaboration platform providing task tracking, project timelines, workflow automation, goals tracking, and team coordination tools. Serves ~150,000 paying customers from SMBs to enterprises including Amazon, Google, and Johnson & Johnson. Founded by Facebook co-founder Dustin Moskovitz. Revenue of ~$723M in FY2026 (ending Jan 2026). Market cap ~$1.5B, down from ~$28B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: AI agents and copilots are structurally undermining the value of standalone project management SaaS. AI can now autonomously create project plans, assign tasks, generate status updates, triage incoming requests, and manage workflows—core functions that justify Asana's per-seat pricing. The per-seat model is under direct threat as AI 'teammates' handle work that previously required human project managers, compressing the number of seats enterprises need. Stock has fallen 60% from $17.21 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$6.86, and is down 92% from its 2021 peak of ~$145. Net retention rate dropped to 95-96%—below 100%, meaning existing customers are actively downsizing—reflecting persistent churn and downgrades across enterprise and mid-market segments. Revenue growth decelerated from 45%+ (FY2023) to ~9% (Q4 FY2026), with Q4 FY2026 revenue of $205.6M missing analyst consensus. Co-founder CEO Dustin Moskovitz stepped down in late 2025. Asana was named alongside Sprout Social and Five9 in Jefferies' 'SaaSpocalypse' report as SaaS companies most exposed to AI-driven seat compression. The company is pivoting aggressively with AI Studio (no-code AI workflow builder) and AI Teammates (autonomous agents), but faces a cannibalization paradox: the better its AI features work, the fewer human seats customers need. Meanwhile, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, and AI-native competitors are embedding project management capabilities directly into productivity suites, threatening Asana's standalone value proposition.

name: EPAM Systems Inc
description: Global leader in digital platform engineering and software development outsourcing. Headquartered in Pennsylvania with ~55,000 engineers across 50+ countries including major delivery centers in Eastern Europe, India, and Latin America. Revenue of ~$5.5B in 2025. Serves Fortune 500 clients across financial services, healthcare, life sciences, and technology sectors.
disruption: The bellwether for AI disruption of the IT services outsourcing industry. AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude) enable enterprise clients to accomplish more software development with fewer outsourced engineers, directly threatening the headcount-based billing model. Stock fell 59% from ~$344 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$131 in Feb 2026. Despite beating Q4 2025 earnings estimates, stock crashed 21% in a single day (Feb 20, 2026) after management guided for just 4.5-7.5% revenue growth in 2026—a dramatic deceleration from 15% in 2025. AI-related revenues ($600M projected for 2026) are growing but cannot offset the structural decline in traditional time-and-materials software development contracts. The broader 2026 software selloff has amplified fears that AI will permanently shrink the total addressable market for outsourced IT services.

name: TripAdvisor Inc
description: Global online travel platform and reviews aggregator with over 1 billion reviews and opinions covering hotels, restaurants, experiences, and attractions. Historically the world's largest travel guidance site, built on search-driven traffic from Google. Revenue of ~$1.9B in 2025. Monthly visits dropped from 146-169M in early 2023 to ~120M by early 2025. Cut ~20% of workforce (~450 people) in 2025 restructuring.
disruption: Google AI Overviews and AI-powered travel planning tools are destroying TripAdvisor's core traffic engine. The company's legacy business model depends on users arriving via Google search to read reviews and click through to book hotels—but AI now answers travel queries directly in search results, eliminating the click-through. CEO Matt Goldberg explicitly cited 'ongoing declines in flyby visitors due to the changing search landscape and the rise of AI overviews,' while CMO Matthew Dacey warned that 'Google's AI mode in search is going to eat large chunks of search.' Stock has fallen ~49% from $19.58 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$10.07. The hotel metasearch segment—the legacy core—declined 14% in Q4 2025, with management guiding for continued mid-to-high teens revenue declines in Hotels & Other through 2026. Zero-click searches now account for 69% of all queries, and AI Overviews jumped from 10-15% of search results to nearly 60% in 2025. The company is pivoting to Experiences (Viator) and dining (TheFork), which now comprise two-thirds of revenue, but the original reviews-and-metasearch business that defined TripAdvisor is in structural decline. Activist investors Starboard Value and Palliser Capital are pushing for asset sales or a full company sale, signaling that the standalone public company model may not survive the AI-driven erosion of search-referral travel traffic.

name: Ziff Davis Inc
description: Global digital media company owning technology and lifestyle brands including PCMag, Mashable, Lifehacker, IGN, and Offers.com. Business model heavily reliant on search-driven affiliate commerce—users arrive via Google search, read product reviews, and click affiliate links to purchase. Also operates Ookla (Speedtest) and health/martech divisions. Revenue ~$1.4B in 2025. Announced $1.2B sale of Connectivity division (Ookla) to Accenture in March 2026.
disruption: AI-powered search is destroying Ziff Davis's core revenue engine. Google AI Overviews and ChatGPT now answer product comparison and tech recommendation queries directly, eliminating the high-intent search traffic that drove users to PCMag reviews and affiliate purchase links. The Technology & Shopping segment—the company's largest—declined 18% YoY in Q4 2025 ($108.9M vs $132.9M), and management expects continued double-digit declines in 2026. CEO Vivek Shah acknowledged that AI search traffic loss is essentially irreplaceable: the high-intent consumers who arrived via search to click through and purchase are simply gone. Stock has fallen 52% from $88.36 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$42.41 (and was as low as ~$27 before a temporary bounce from the Ookla asset sale). Q4 2025 earnings missed estimates, triggering a 12% single-day drop. The $1.2B Connectivity sale to Accenture—at a price exceeding the company's entire pre-deal market cap—signals management is liquidating its healthiest non-AI-disrupted assets to return capital, tacitly conceding that the core digital media business faces structural AI headwinds.

name: TaskUs Inc
description: Global digital outsourcing company providing content moderation (trust & safety), customer experience, and AI data annotation services to major tech companies. Largest client is Meta (~27% of revenue). Operations across 28 countries with ~47,000 employees. Named a Leader in Everest Group's Data Annotation and Labeling PEAK Matrix. Revenue hit a record $1.18B in 2025. Market cap ~$900M, down from ~$3B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: AI is disrupting TaskUs from multiple directions simultaneously. Its largest client, Meta (27% of revenue), has explicitly signaled it will leverage AI to automate content moderation and reduce outsourced work volumes in 2026—directly contributing to TaskUs's guidance of just 3.5% revenue growth, a dramatic deceleration from 19% in 2025. AI-powered moderation tools can increasingly handle the trust & safety work (flagging harmful content, financial crime compliance) that human moderators perform, and management acknowledged trust & safety will likely have the lowest growth rate in 2026 'given the impact that automation is likely to have on core content moderation volumes.' Stock has fallen 45% from $18.73 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$10.20, and dropped 39% in the six months following record 2025 earnings as investors priced in structural AI headwinds. TaskUs faces a deeper paradox in its AI data annotation business: while AI Services grew 46% in 2025, the improving capability of AI models means they increasingly need less human-labeled training data (via synthetic data, self-supervision, and RLHF improvements), potentially shrinking the very segment that is supposed to offset legacy declines. The company is investing $25M+ in AI transformation and pivoting to outcome-based pricing, but the fundamental tension remains—TaskUs's human-labor-intensive model is being squeezed by the same AI technology it helps train and moderate.

name: Freshworks Inc
description: Cloud-based customer support and IT service management (ITSM) SaaS platform offering helpdesk ticketing, CRM, and IT operations tools for small and mid-market businesses. Products include Freshdesk (customer support), Freshservice (ITSM), and Freshsales (CRM). ~4,500 employees. Revenue of ~$839M in FY2025 with ~$907M ARR. Market cap ~$2.3B, down from ~$12B at IPO peak in 2021.
disruption: A poster child of the February 2026 'SaaSpocalypse'—the $285B+ enterprise software selloff triggered by fears that AI agents will structurally break the per-seat SaaS business model. AI chatbots and autonomous agents can now resolve customer support tickets end-to-end, reducing the number of human agents who need Freshworks' helpdesk software licenses. AI coding tools ('vibe coding') enable businesses to build custom support workflows without buying off-the-shelf SaaS. Jefferies downgraded FRSH to Hold, specifically citing AI disruption risk to the customer experience business. Piper Sandler also downgraded on fears of seat compression as AI replaces human support agents. Stock has fallen 41% from $14.05 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$8.23, and is down 95% from its 2021 IPO peak. Revenue growth has decelerated from 20%+ (2023) to ~14% (2025), with 2026 profit guidance missing estimates. While Freshworks is investing in its own AI agent platform (Freddy AI, achieving 70% ticket deflection across 5,000+ organizations), the fundamental threat is that AI makes the per-seat helpdesk model less valuable—if one AI agent handles the work of five human agents, the total seats needed shrinks dramatically, compressing the revenue base for all customer support SaaS vendors.

name: Wolters Kluwer NV
description: Global information services company providing software and expert solutions for healthcare, tax & accounting, financial services, compliance, and legal professionals. Deep domain expertise embedded in workflow tools.
disruption: The February 2026 selloff in legal and professional-information stocks showed that investors see agentic AI as a threat to premium research, drafting, tax, and compliance workflows. Wolters Kluwer is better insulated than many peers because its proprietary content is embedded in regulated professional workflows, and current results do not show an AI-driven revenue decline; its own AI products may strengthen retention. The qualifying pressure is longer-term pricing and seat risk where general-purpose agents can perform parts of the workflow, balanced by strong data, trust, and distribution advantages.

name: Adobe Inc
description: Software giant providing creative tools (Photoshop, Illustrator, Premiere Pro), document management (Acrobat), and digital marketing solutions. Dominant position in professional creative workflows.
disruption: Generative image, video, and design tools have lowered the cost of producing acceptable creative work and created credible alternatives for casual and entry-level users, directly compressing the scarcity value behind parts of Adobe's suite. Adobe's stock has been repriced on that risk, but current operating evidence is resilient: subscription revenue and ARR are still growing at double-digit rates and Firefly is generating AI-led demand. The thesis is competitive and pricing pressure at the edge of the franchise, not a present collapse of Creative Cloud; professional workflows, installed-base depth, and commercially safe AI remain important offsets.

name: Genpact Ltd
description: Global professional services firm providing business process management and IT services, with focus on digital transformation, analytics, and enterprise AI. Serves Fortune 500 companies across finance, insurance, and manufacturing.
disruption: AI can automate document processing, accounting, claims, support, and other rules-based work that historically generated labor-based BPO revenue for Genpact. That creates pressure to move from headcount billing to outcome-based services. The evidence is mixed because Genpact is also winning AI implementation and transformation work, and current demand is affected by enterprise budgets and ordinary outsourcing cycles. The retained thesis is compression of legacy labor intensity and pricing, not that Genpact is simply an AI victim.

name: Upwork Inc
description: Global freelancing platform connecting businesses with independent professionals for projects in development, design, writing, marketing, and administrative work. Largest platform for remote freelance work.
disruption: AI tools directly displace freelance work in writing, design, basic coding, data entry, and marketing—the highest-volume categories on Upwork. Businesses can use AI instead of hiring a freelancer for many routine knowledge tasks. Upwork has seen pressure on take rates and gross services volume. Like Fiverr, the platform faces a shrinking middle market where tasks are too simple for human freelancers (AI handles them) but the complex work remains human-intensive.

name: BuzzFeed Inc
description: Digital media company known for listicles, quizzes, news, and entertainment content. Operates BuzzFeed, HuffPost, and Tasty brands. Revenue driven by advertising and content partnerships.
disruption: AI-generated content has flooded the web, directly competing with BuzzFeed's core output of listicles, quizzes, and lightweight articles. Google AI Overviews and AI-powered search reduce click-through traffic to publishers, cratering pageviews and ad revenue. Advertisers increasingly use AI tools to produce branded content in-house rather than partnering with publishers. BuzzFeed's own pivot to AI-generated content (AI quizzes) failed to offset the structural decline. The economics of ad-supported digital media have deteriorated as AI commoditizes content creation.

name: Gartner Inc
description: World's leading IT research and advisory firm providing syndicated research, consulting, and conferences to enterprise technology leaders. Gartner's Magic Quadrants, Hype Cycles, and analyst insights shape billions in IT spending decisions. Revenue of ~$6.3B in FY2025 with ~20,000 employees.
disruption: Generative AI can perform first-pass market synthesis, vendor comparison, and advisory work, weakening the scarcity and visibility premium attached to Gartner research. The February 2026 repricing and softer guidance make that a material investor concern. Current operations do not prove wholesale AI substitution: subscription relationships remain embedded, and sales execution, government and enterprise budgets, conference timing, and macro conditions also affect growth. The thesis is longer-term pressure on research pricing and seat expansion, not that AI alone caused the earnings miss.

name: Conduent Incorporated
description: Global business process services company spun off from Xerox in 2017, providing outsourced digital interactions, transaction processing, and government services. Operates in Commercial, Government, and Transportation segments. Services include HR administration, benefits management, toll collection, payment processing, and customer care. Revenue of ~$3B with ~55,000 employees across 20+ countries.
disruption: AI automation is displacing the human-intensive business process work that forms Conduent's core revenue base. AI-powered document processing, intelligent automation, and chatbots directly replace the manual transaction processing, claims handling, and customer interactions Conduent performs for government and enterprise clients. CEO Harshita Agadi acknowledged that clients may take AI-driven processes in-house, and the company is attempting to pivot by partnering with AI disruptors rather than building its own. Revenue declined ~8% YoY excluding acquisitions in Q4 2025, the company posted a net loss of $170M, and stock has fallen ~68% from its pre-ChatGPT level. The structural threat is clear: the BPO industry's FTE-based billing model is giving way to outcome-based pricing that rewards automation over headcount, with FTE-based contracts projected to fall from 42% to 28% of industry revenue within three years.

name: Atlassian Corporation Plc
description: Australian-American enterprise software company providing collaboration and project management tools including Jira, Confluence, Trello, and Bitbucket. Serves over 300,000 customers globally with a seat-based SaaS model. Revenue of ~$4.8B with ~12,000 employees.
disruption: AI agents can automate parts of software planning, ticket creation, documentation, and support, creating a real long-term risk to Atlassian's per-user expansion model. The 2026 software selloff and analyst downgrades directly repriced that risk. Current results are still strong, with revenue growing above 20%, and Atlassian's own agent platform can increase product value; cloud migration, sales execution, and valuation compression also matter. The thesis is future seat and pricing pressure rather than a demonstrated AI-driven revenue contraction.

name: Capgemini SE
description: French multinational IT services and consulting company, one of the world's largest. Provides consulting, technology services, digital transformation, and outsourcing across 50+ countries. Revenue of ~€22.5B with ~340,000 employees. Competes with Accenture, Infosys, TCS, and Wipro in enterprise IT services.
disruption: AI is structurally disrupting Capgemini's labor-intensive IT services model. Morningstar downgraded the company's moat rating to 'none' in 2026, citing that AI renders the core time-and-materials pricing model obsolete as the primary input—human labor—is being compressed by AI automation. Bookings declined 5% YoY in 2025 as clients delay spending to assess AI alternatives to traditional outsourced services. The company announced €700M in restructuring charges to pivot its workforce toward AI-enabled services, effectively acknowledging the disruption. Revenue growth missed analyst expectations for three consecutive quarters, and offshore pricing power has eroded as AI tools reduce the value proposition of labor arbitrage. The stock has fallen over 40% from its pre-ChatGPT price as investors price in the structural threat to the entire IT services industry.

name: Dassault Systèmes SE
description: French multinational software company specializing in 3D design, simulation, and product lifecycle management (PLM). Provides the 3DEXPERIENCE platform, CATIA, SOLIDWORKS, and other CAD/engineering tools used across aerospace, automotive, and manufacturing. Revenue of ~€6.4B with ~24,000 employees.
disruption: Generative design and engineering agents can automate portions of modeling, simulation, and product-development work, increasing pressure on Dassault Systèmes' seat-based licensing and prompting interest in usage-based AI pricing. The sharp 2026 market repricing reflects that risk, but weak results also came from industrial demand, China and geographic exposure, Life Sciences performance, transaction timing, and execution. Current evidence does not show broad replacement of 3DEXPERIENCE; the retained thesis is structural workflow and pricing pressure with substantial non-AI co-drivers.

name: Infosys Limited
description: Indian multinational IT services and consulting company, one of the world's largest offshore IT outsourcers. Provides consulting, technology, and outsourcing services across 50+ countries. Revenue of ~$19B with ~320,000 employees. Competes with TCS, Wipro, Accenture, and Capgemini in enterprise IT services.
disruption: AI coding and application-maintenance agents can reduce the effort and headcount required for the application services that form a large part of Infosys's delivery model. That threatens labor-arbitrage pricing even as it creates new AI implementation work. Recent weakness is mixed: delayed discretionary spending, client budget caution, sector cycles, currency, and execution remain important, and management reports meaningful AI demand. The thesis is compression of legacy service-line revenue and team size, not that all current results are AI-caused.

name: CS Disco Inc
description: Legal technology company providing cloud-based eDiscovery, document review, and case management software for law firms, corporations, and government agencies. Founded in 2012, IPO'd in 2021. Revenue of ~$165M (2025) with ~500 employees. Competes with Relativity, Reveal, Nuix, and emerging AI-native legal platforms like Harvey and Legora.
disruption: AI is disrupting CS Disco's core eDiscovery and document review business. Generative AI tools can now analyze, summarize, and review legal documents far faster and cheaper than Disco's traditional keyword-and-analytics approach, commoditizing the legal review workflows that drive Disco's revenue. AI-native legal startups like Harvey (valued at 110x ARR vs. Disco's ~1x) and Legora are attracting enterprise legal budgets—Disco's stock plunged 20% on news of Legora's launch. The February 2026 SaaSpocalypse hit legal tech particularly hard after Anthropic released legal-focused AI tools, triggering a repricing of the entire legal software sector. While Disco launched its own AI assistant (Cecilia AI) with 300%+ adoption growth, the company remains unprofitable and revenue growth (~13% YoY) lags AI-native competitors. The stock has fallen 48% below its pre-ChatGPT price as the market prices in structural disruption to traditional legal technology platforms.

name: NICE Ltd
description: Israeli-founded enterprise software company providing cloud and on-premises contact center solutions including workforce management, quality management, analytics, and AI-powered virtual agents. A market leader in the CCaaS (Contact Center as a Service) space alongside Genesys and Five9, serving ~25,000 organizations globally with ~$2.7B annual revenue and ~8,000 employees. Market cap ~$6.8B, down from a ~$20B peak in 2022.
disruption: AI voice and service agents can reduce the human contact-center seats that support NICE's legacy CXone monetization. NICE's acquisition of Cognigy and move toward AI-agent pricing are a strategic response, but they also create near-term margin and integration pressure. Current revenue still benefits from AI adoption, and competition, acquisition accounting, sales cycles, and the planned profitability reset are material co-drivers. The thesis is cannibalization risk to the human-seat base, not a present AI-driven collapse.

name: Kforce Inc
description: Technology and finance & accounting staffing firm providing temporary and direct-hire professionals to Fortune 500 and mid-market clients across the US. Specializes in placing IT contractors including software developers, data scientists, project managers, and cybersecurity professionals. Revenue of ~$1.4B (2025, down 5.4% YoY) with ~2,000 internal employees placing ~8,000+ consultants. Competes with ASGN (Apex Group), Robert Half (RHI), ManpowerGroup (MAN), and other IT staffing firms.
disruption: AI coding tools can reduce the number of contract developers needed for a given project, creating a structural risk to Kforce's billable technology headcount. The recent staffing downturn was nevertheless driven mainly by the low-hire macro environment and client caution, and first-quarter 2026 revenue returned to slight growth. Kforce is retained because its filings now identify AI-driven demand shifts as a specific risk, but the narrative no longer presents AI as the sole cause of the cyclical decline.

name: Angi Inc
description: Home services marketplace formed from the 2017 merger of Angie's List and HomeAdvisor under IAC (rebranded to Angi in 2021). Connects homeowners with service professionals (contractors, plumbers, electricians) for home improvement, maintenance, and repair projects. Revenue of ~$1.03B in 2025, down 13% YoY. Approximately 2,800 employees before cutting 350 (~12.5%) in January 2026. Market cap ~$311M, down from over $4B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: Google AI Overviews and AI-powered search have directly crushed Angi's traffic-dependent business model—the same AI disruption devastating TripAdvisor and Ziff Davis. Angi's legacy revenue engine depends on homeowners arriving via Google search to read contractor reviews and submit service requests, but AI now answers 'who's the best plumber near me' type queries directly in search results, eliminating the click-through. Management explicitly cited 'continued declines in Google SEO and network channels' as central to the Q4 2025 revenue shortfall, as algorithm changes and AI Overviews have reduced free traffic. Organic click-through rates for informational queries with AI Overviews have fallen 61% since mid-2024. Simultaneously, users are increasingly turning to ChatGPT and Nextdoor for contractor recommendations, bypassing Angi's pay-to-play directory entirely. In January 2026, Angi announced 350 layoffs (~12.5% of workforce) explicitly citing 'AI-driven efficiency improvements' to save $70-80M annually—acknowledging that AI is reshaping both its cost structure and its top-line. Stock fell 65% on a split-adjusted basis from $20.60 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$7.20, and hit an all-time low of $6.43 in March 2026. The company executed a 1:10 reverse stock split in March 2025 and is attempting an AI-powered pivot (AI helper in service request flow with 35% homeowner engagement and 3.3x conversion), but faces the 'shrinking to profitability' dilemma as revenue continues declining faster than management can re-engineer the business for an AI-first search landscape.

name: Freelancer Limited
description: Sydney-headquartered operator of Freelancer.com, the world's largest freelance marketplace by user count with ~80M registered users across 247 countries and territories, plus Escrow.com (online payments) and Loadshift (Australian freight marketplace). Founder Matt Barrie remains CEO. FY2025 results delivered a 'return to profitability' driven mainly by Escrow.com and Loadshift rather than the core marketplace, with Q3 2025 group GMV collapsing 22.5% to $206.8M as the platform lapped an extraordinary prior-year transaction. Market cap of ~A$70M, trading near the bottom of its A$0.150-0.325 52-week range.
disruption: Freelancer's three highest-volume marketplace categories — writing, software/web development, and design — are exactly the categories where AI is automating work fastest. Industry data for 2025 shows writing project demand down ~30% YoY, software/web development down ~20%, and entry-level writing falling from 15% to under 9% of jobs — the same dynamic pressuring FVRR and UPWK on this list. Stock has fallen ~48% from A$0.26 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~A$0.135. The profit recovery in 2025 came almost entirely from adjacent non-marketplace businesses (Escrow.com and Loadshift), tacitly acknowledging that the core freelance platform is under structural pressure from AI. Management is leaning into AI as a category itself — positioning AI development as 'the next major wave after web and mobile' — and adding AI-assisted matching, quality scoring, and audio/video calling features to defend retention, but the same LLMs creating new AI engineering jobs are eliminating the much larger volume of routine writing, coding, and design gigs that built Freelancer's GMV. Founder Matt Barrie has been dollar-averaging into the weakness (673,076 shares bought on Dec 5, 2025 at ~A$0.23), a personal bet that the marketplace can survive the AI transition.

name: PagerDuty Inc
description: Digital operations management platform providing real-time incident alerting, on-call scheduling, event intelligence, and automated response orchestration for engineering and IT teams. Serves ~29,000 customers including over half the Fortune 500. Revenue of ~$499M (FY2026) growing just 2.7% YoY with ~1,000 employees. Achieved first full year of GAAP profitability in FY2026, but revenue growth has stalled. Competes with Opsgenie (Atlassian), Datadog Incident Management, and emerging AI-native operations tools.
disruption: AI agents are directly threatening PagerDuty's core incident management business. Autonomous AI agents can now auto-triage alerts, diagnose root causes, and remediate issues without human intervention — the exact workflow PagerDuty monetizes through per-seat pricing for on-call engineers. Anthropic's Managed Agents announcement triggered an 8.7% single-day drop as traders recognized that autonomous AI systems could replace human-operated incident response tools with more efficient, autonomous infrastructure. The February 2026 SaaSpocalypse compounded the decline, with PD falling 24.8% over 7 days and 36.8% over 30 days as investors repriced the entire seat-based SaaS model. Growing use of AI automation and open-source tools threatens PagerDuty's pricing power, market size, and customer retention. Despite expanding its AI ecosystem with 30+ partners and launching PagerDuty Advance agents for autonomous cloud remediation via MCP and API integrations, the market views PagerDuty's core workflow — alert triage, on-call routing, and incident coordination — as among the most automatable enterprise tasks for AI agents. Stock has fallen 72% below its pre-ChatGPT price, hitting new lows in April 2026 as the AI agent wave intensifies.

name: Rapid7 Inc
description: Cloud-based cybersecurity platform providing vulnerability management, managed detection and response (MDR), security information and event management (SIEM), and cloud security solutions. Serves ~11,000 customers globally. Revenue of ~$842M (2025) with growth stalling near flat YoY. Approximately 2,400 employees. Competes with CrowdStrike, Tenable, Qualys, and Palo Alto Networks in vulnerability management and detection/response.
disruption: Claude Mythos and other autonomous security tools can perform vulnerability discovery and parts of assessment work that specialists monetize, increasing commoditization risk for Rapid7. The same technology also creates more vulnerabilities and demand for exposure management, so AI is not a one-way headwind. Rapid7's flat growth and weak guidance also reflect execution, specialist-versus-platform competition, and customer budgets. The retained thesis is pressure on point-solution pricing and differentiation, not that Mythos alone caused the decline.

name: Yext Inc
description: Cloud-based digital presence platform that manages business listings, reviews, and search optimization across 200+ publisher networks including Google, Apple, Bing, and Facebook. Helps brands control their name, address, phone, hours, and other critical business information across the internet. Also provides website search, review monitoring, and analytics tools. Revenue of ~$448M (FY2026 ending Jan 2026), with headline growth of 6% driven entirely by the Hearsay acquisition—organic revenue declined 2%. ~1,120 employees after 8% workforce cuts in 2023 and 12% in 2024. Market cap ~$500M.
disruption: AI-powered search is structurally eroding the value of Yext's core digital presence management platform. As AI chatbots (ChatGPT, Google AI Overviews, Perplexity) answer local and business queries directly, consumers no longer click through search results to find business listings—making the painstaking management of listings across dozens of directories less valuable. 45% of consumers now use AI for local recommendations, up from 6% just one year ago—a 650% increase—and Gartner projects search engine volume will drop 25% by 2026. Yext's organic revenue has declined for two consecutive years—down 4% in FY2025 and 2% in FY2026 (excluding the Hearsay acquisition). ARR stagnated at $444.3M, with small customer ARR collapsing 14.3% YoY as SMBs increasingly find that AI makes listing management less necessary. Net revenue retention dropped to 86%, meaning existing customers are actively shrinking their spend. Roth Capital downgraded the stock after subpar Q4 results (revenue $112M, -1% YoY), and Seeking Alpha downgraded to Sell citing 'persistent revenue declines, ARR stagnation, and customer churn.' The company suspended forward guidance and launched a $140M tender offer to buy back shares at depressed prices. Stock has fallen 31% from $5.26 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$3.62. While Yext is attempting to pivot from 'search optimization' to 'AI optimization'—helping brands appear in AI-generated responses rather than traditional search results—this new market is nascent and the company faces a painful transition as its legacy search listing revenue erodes faster than AI-era revenue can replace it.

name: Nerdy Inc
description: Online learning platform operating Varsity Tutors, offering live one-on-one tutoring, small group classes, large-format courses, and AI-assisted study tools across 3,000+ subjects for K-12 students, college learners, and adult professionals. Founded in 2011, went public via SPAC in 2021 at a ~$1.7B valuation. Revenue of ~$179M in FY2025, down 5.91% YoY from ~$190M. Cut 22% of workforce. Received NYSE non-compliance notice in March 2026 for trading below $1.00. Market cap ~$70M, down from ~$1.7B at SPAC peak.
disruption: ChatGPT and free AI tutoring tools have directly displaced the paid human tutoring that is Nerdy's core business—the same AI disruption that destroyed Chegg's homework help model. Students who once paid $40-80/hour for a Varsity Tutors session can now get instant, conversational explanations from ChatGPT, Claude, or Google Gemini for free. A Harvard Crimson student reported using ChatGPT as a personal tutor for an entire year, and industry analysis projects AI will replace the vast majority of traditional tutoring use cases by 2026. Revenue declined 5.91% in FY2025 ($190M to $179M) after years of growth, and the company cut 22% of its workforce to survive. Stock has fallen 55% from $2.10 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$0.94, triggering an NYSE non-compliance notice in March 2026 for sustained sub-$1 trading—the company previously regained compliance in December 2024 but fell below again, suggesting the decline is structural rather than temporary. Nerdy had to completely rebuild its platform as 'AI-native' (Live+AI), a tacit admission that the pre-ChatGPT tutoring model was broken—mirroring LivePerson's failed attempt to pivot from legacy chatbots to LLM-powered solutions. Q4 2025 showed a brief 2% revenue uptick but Q1 2026 guidance of $46-48M implies flat growth at best, and Danelfin rates the stock a 3/10 Sell. The Department of Education's uncertain future under the current administration adds additional headwinds. While Nerdy positions AI as enhancing rather than replacing human tutors, the market has rendered its verdict: when AI provides comparable academic support for free, the willingness to pay for human tutoring collapses.

name: Domo Inc
description: Cloud-based business intelligence and data analytics platform providing dashboards, data visualization, enterprise data integration, and embedded analytics for mid-market and enterprise customers. Founded in 2011 by Josh James (former Omniture CEO), IPO'd in 2018. Revenue of ~$317M in FY2026 (ending Jan 2026), essentially flat YoY. ~800 employees. Carries $126M in convertible debt at 12.2% interest with only ~$43M cash. Market cap ~$120M. Board launched formal strategic alternatives review in February 2026, exploring a potential sale or business combination, with Jefferies as financial adviser.
disruption: AI is making Domo's core business intelligence platform obsolete. Seeking Alpha published analysis titled 'Domo: AI Is Making This Company Obsolete,' arguing that ChatGPT, Claude, and other AI tools can now perform the ad-hoc data analysis, dashboard creation, and business insight generation that previously required dedicated BI platforms like Domo. AI coding tools enable companies to build custom analytics workflows in hours rather than purchasing off-the-shelf BI SaaS subscriptions. Revenue has stagnated at ~$317M with guidance for a ~1% decline—a plateau that reflects structural displacement rather than a growth slowdown. Stock has fallen 72% from $13.41 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$3.80, and is down 86.5% from its 52-week high. DA Davidson cut its price target from $6 to $3.50, and Citizens maintains a Market Underperform rating with a $3.50 target. The founder/CEO stepped back from day-to-day operations. The February 2026 strategic alternatives review—exploring a sale of the entire company—is the clearest signal of existential distress, acknowledging that Domo cannot survive independently as AI commoditizes its core BI functionality. While billings and backlog showed 8% YoY growth in Q4 and the company is pivoting to an AI Agent Builder and MCP Server platform, these nascent AI initiatives cannot offset the fundamental reality that generative AI has collapsed the barrier to entry for business analytics—the very market Domo spent over a decade and $2B+ in cumulative losses trying to dominate.

name: Reach plc
description: UK's largest commercial news publisher, operating national and regional titles including the Daily Mirror, Daily Express, Daily Star, Manchester Evening News, and Liverpool Echo, plus over 100 local news brands. Generates revenue from print advertising, digital advertising, and increasingly subscriptions and content licensing. Revenue of £518.4M in 2025, down 3.7% YoY, split between print (£388.1M, -4.6%) and digital (£128.9M, -0.9%). Approximately 4,000 employees. Market cap ~£200M, down from ~£700M at its 2019 peak.
disruption: Google AI Overviews and AI-powered search are destroying Reach's digital revenue engine—the same 'AI search death spiral' devastating BuzzFeed (BZFD), Ziff Davis (ZD), and TripAdvisor (TRIP) on this list. Google traffic to Reach's sites plunged 46% in 2025, driven by changes to Google Discover and AI Overviews that answer user queries directly without sending clicks to publishers. NPR called the phenomenon an 'extinction-level event' for online publishers. When an AI Overview appears at the top of Google search results, just 1% of users click through to the cited links (Pew Research). Globally, organic Google search traffic to publishers fell 33% from November 2024 to November 2025. Digital revenue at Reach dropped 7.8% YoY in Q4 2025 as the traffic collapse accelerated, and management warned of 'lower digital revenue expectations for 2026 attributable to the decline in referral traffic.' The company posted a statutory operating loss of £160.1M in 2025, driven by a £222.8M non-cash impairment charge—an implicit write-down of the value of digital media assets in an AI-disrupted world. Over 600 roles are at risk as Reach restructures, closing two of its three print facilities. Stock has fallen 44% from 119.28p on Nov 29, 2022 to ~67.00p. Reach is pivoting to subscriptions (targeting 75,000 by end of 2026), deploying an AI answer engine (DeeperDive from Taboola) directly on its sites, and earning revenue from Meta's Content Monetization program, but these nascent revenue streams cannot yet offset the structural erosion of search-referral advertising—the business model that sustained digital publishing for two decades.

name: Kainos Group plc
description: Belfast-headquartered digital transformation and IT services company operating three segments: Digital Services (custom software development and consulting for public sector and enterprise clients), Workday Services (implementation and optimization consulting for Workday HCM platform), and Workday Products (proprietary SaaS plugins for Workday). Operations span UK, Ireland, Europe, and North America with ~3,000 employees. Revenue of £367.2M in FY2025 (ending March 2025), down 4% YoY. Adjusted pre-tax profit of £65.6M, down 15%. Acquired Davis Pier (Canadian consulting firm, 120 employees) in September 2025. Market cap ~£550M, down from ~£3B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: AI coding tools are structurally reducing demand for the outsourced custom software development that forms Kainos's core Digital Services business—the same thesis disrupting EPAM (-59%), Endava (-93%), Globant (-74%), and Capgemini (-40%) on this list. Digital Services revenue declined 7% to £197.2M in FY2025, and the company cut 190 staff (~6% of workforce) in March 2025 to reallocate resources toward AI and product development. The stock crashed 40% in just three months from December 2025 through February 2026 as the SaaSpocalypse sell-off hit IT services stocks particularly hard—with The Motley Fool framing the key investor question as whether Kainos is 'an AI enabler or an AI victim' whose 'core consulting-style revenues are at risk.' P/E fell to a decade low of 14.8. Deutsche Bank downgraded to Hold. While AI projects surged 61% to £41.1M and the company launched a Microsoft AI Centre of Excellence with 250+ AI professionals, these AI revenues cannot yet offset the structural erosion of traditional consulting-style Digital Services contracts as AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Claude Code) enable clients to accomplish more development with fewer outsourced engineers. H1 FY2026 showed early recovery signs (revenue +7%, bookings +27%), but the stock remains deeply below its pre-ChatGPT level as investors price in permanent compression of the IT services TAM.

name: Future plc
description: UK-based digital media company operating ~230 specialist brands including TechRadar, Tom's Guide, PC Gamer, GamesRadar+, Marie Claire, and Tom's Hardware. Unlike traditional news publishers, Future's business model is built on high-margin affiliate ecommerce commissions and programmatic display advertising—readers arrive via Google search to read product reviews and buying guides, then click through to purchase via affiliate links. Revenue of £788.2M in FY2024 (ending Sep 2024), flat YoY. Online audience declined from 241M to 226M users. EBITDA margins compressed from ~30% in FY2025 to a guided 24-25% for H1 FY2026. ~2,991 employees. Market cap ~£316M, down from ~£5B at its 2021 peak.
disruption: Google AI Overviews are destroying Future's highest-margin revenue streams—the same 'AI search death spiral' devastating Ziff Davis (ZD), TripAdvisor (TRIP), Angi (ANGI), BuzzFeed (BZFD), and Reach (RCH.L) on this list. Future's entire business model depends on Google search traffic driving readers to product reviews and buying guides where they click affiliate links to purchase—but AI Overviews now answer product comparison queries ('best laptop 2026,' 'top wireless headphones') directly in search results, eliminating the click-through. Research shows AI Overviews cause a 47.5% drop in click-through rates on desktop and 37.7% on mobile, with sites previously ranking first seeing ~79% traffic declines when placed beneath an AI Overview. Stock has fallen 77% from 1482.36p on Nov 29, 2022 to ~344p. In April 2026, Future issued a profit warning cutting full-year EBITDA margin guidance from ~30% to 24-25%, citing Google search traffic declines 'more pronounced than expected' that disproportionately hit higher-margin programmatic advertising and ecommerce affiliate revenues. Barclays downgraded the stock and slashed its price target 55% (from 640p to 285p), citing 'dual structural headwinds from Agentic AI and New Media' and warning that Future's 'highest-margin revenue streams face significant AI disintermediation risks.' FY26 EPS estimates were cut 20% and FY28 estimates cut 36%. Organic revenue declined 6.5% YoY in H1 FY2025 with audience dropping 20%. The company is closing underperforming magazine titles and investing £25-30M in a Growth Acceleration Strategy pivoting toward direct audience relationships (newsletters, Apple News), but these lower-margin channels cannot replace the high-margin affiliate and programmatic revenue that Google search traffic once delivered.

name: Resources Connection Inc
description: Professional consulting and staffing firm placing finance, accounting, governance, risk & compliance, and business transformation consultants on project-based engagements at Fortune 500 and large enterprise clients. Operates three segments: On-Demand Talent (contract staffing), Consulting (Veracity and countermeasure brands), and Europe & Asia Pacific. Revenue of ~$551M in FY2025 (ending May 2025), down ~13% YoY. Q3 FY2026 revenue $107.9M, down 19.6% YoY. Revenue declining for 7+ consecutive quarters. Levered free cash flow fell 76% YoY. ~3,000 employees.
disruption: AI tools are directly reducing demand for the contract consulting and operational accounting roles that form RGP's core On-Demand Talent business—the same disruption hitting staffing peers Robert Half (RHI), ManpowerGroup (MAN), Kforce (KFRC), and Kelly Services (KELYA) on this list. RGP's CEO explicitly acknowledged on earnings calls that AI is reducing client demand for operational accounting contract roles, as AI-powered automation handles tasks that previously required temporary finance and accounting professionals. The On-Demand Talent segment saw a 33% revenue decline in a single quarter—the worst-performing segment. Seeking Alpha downgraded RGP to Sell citing 'structural threats from automation' alongside demand softness. An ainvest.com analysis of Q3 FY2026 earnings noted that 'AI's role contradicts prior optimism'—the company had previously positioned AI as a tailwind but is now seeing it cannibalize traditional staffing demand. Stock has fallen 75% from $16.12 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$4.05. While RGP is attempting to pivot toward higher-value consulting and AI-enabled transformation services, the core staffing model faces structural headwinds as enterprises deploy AI tools to perform the operational, analytical, and transactional work that contract consultants once handled.

name: BlackLine Inc
description: Cloud-based accounting automation platform providing financial close management, account reconciliations, journal entry management, intercompany accounting, and compliance solutions for enterprise finance teams. Serves ~4,400 customers globally including major enterprises across manufacturing, financial services, technology, and healthcare. Revenue of ~$653M in FY2025 with ARR of ~$702M (+10% YoY). ~1,800 employees. Market cap ~$1.7B, down from ~$10B at its 2021 peak. Competes with FloQast, DOKKA, Oracle, SAP, and Sage in the financial close automation market.
disruption: AI-native competitors are commoditizing BlackLine's core financial close workflows. FloQast (~3,500 customers, $1.6B valuation) undercuts BlackLine at roughly one-third the price (~$12-23K/year vs. BlackLine's ~$77K/year average) while offering AI-powered close automation that appeals to mid-market finance teams. DOKKA has built an AI-first accounting close platform from the ground up. Meanwhile, ERP vendors (Oracle, SAP, Sage) are embedding AI-driven close automation directly into their financial suites, reducing the need for a standalone third-party platform. BlackLine's market share fell from 24.4% to 18.2% in a single year as these AI-powered alternatives erode its position. Net revenue retention dropped to 92% overall (95% enterprise) in Q4 2025, meaning existing customers are actively shrinking their spend. Customer count declined by 30 in Q4 2025 to 4,394. Revenue growth decelerated to 8% YoY in Q4 2025, and Q1 FY2026 EPS guidance of $0.44-0.46 missed analyst consensus of $0.55. Stock has fallen 52% from $63.86 on Nov 29, 2022 to ~$30.50. The 2026 SaaSpocalypse sell-off hit BlackLine especially hard as the AI seat compression narrative took hold: when AI agents can automate account reconciliations, journal entries, and close checklists, enterprises need fewer human users on the platform. Multiple analyst downgrades: Citigroup cut its price target from $70 to $60, BMO to $44, and DA Davidson to $45, with only 44% of analysts maintaining bullish ratings. BlackLine is investing in its own AI features (agentic AI for close automation) but faces the classic incumbent dilemma: its per-seat pricing model depends on human accountants performing the manual close tasks that AI is designed to eliminate.

name: Appen Limited
description: Sydney-headquartered provider of human-annotated training data for AI and machine learning models. Supplies large-scale labeled datasets, multilingual content evaluation, and model validation services to technology companies building AI systems. Founded in 1996 and was once a key supplier to Google, Meta, Microsoft, and Amazon for training their AI models. Revenue declined from US$270M (2023) to US$231M (2025). Posted a US$19.3M net loss in H1 2025. Market cap ~A$500M, down from ~A$4B at its 2020 peak. ~800 employees plus a global crowd workforce.
disruption: Synthetic data, self-supervision, and more capable models reduce demand for some legacy, high-volume human annotation work, pressuring the business Appen built before generative AI. The loss of Google's large contract and declining legacy revenue are important evidence of model-training procurement changing, but the claim that Google simply replaced Appen with its own AI is not established. Customer concentration, contract losses, execution, and restructuring are major co-drivers, while model evaluation, safety, and high-quality human feedback create new demand. Appen is therefore a mixed transition case, not a pure AI-substitution story.

name: Teleperformance SE
description: French-headquartered global customer experience and business process outsourcing leader, now branding as TP. Operates outsourced customer care, technical support, back-office, trust and safety, interpreting, and digital CX services across 100 countries with roughly 490,000 employees. The company generated EUR 10.2B of revenue in 2025 and remains one of the largest human-agent contact center operators in the world.
disruption: Generative AI directly threatens Teleperformance's labor-intensive contact center model by automating the routine customer service conversations that historically required large teams of outsourced human agents. The market made the disruption explicit in February 2024 when Teleperformance shares plunged 19-29% in a single day after Klarna said its OpenAI-powered customer service assistant handled two-thirds of its chats, equivalent to the work of 700 full-time agents and expected to drive a $40M profit improvement. Reuters reported the move was linked to AI disruption worries, and Morgan Stanley noted that fears of AI disintermediation were weighing on BPO growth expectations and valuations. Teleperformance had already said 20-30% of its activities could be automated within three years, then had to downplay the speed of adoption. The company is now pivoting hard toward TP.ai FAB, 500+ AI projects, an AI-native customer operations CEO, and a Chief AI Officer, but the pivot underscores the core problem: clients can increasingly use AI agents to reduce outsourced seat volumes. Q1 2026 revenue declined 2.2% like-for-like, with continued automation in Trust & Safety and offshoring pressure partially offset by AI-powered solutions. Stock has fallen about 71% from EUR 214.00 on Nov 29, 2022 to roughly EUR 61.74, with no relevant reverse split since the 2000 and 2006 historical split adjustments.

name: Hays plc
description: UK-headquartered global specialist recruitment and workforce solutions company placing candidates in temporary, contracting, and permanent roles across technology, accountancy and finance, construction, engineering, life sciences, and other professional markets. Operates in 30+ countries with Hays-branded recruitment consultants and employer/candidate platforms. FY2025 net fee income was GBP 972.4M, down 11% year over year, with operating profit sharply lower as the company restructured for a prolonged recruitment downturn.
disruption: Generative AI directly threatens Hays's white-collar recruitment model from both sides: employers need fewer entry-level and back-office workers as AI automates routine office tasks, and AI recruiting tools let clients screen resumes, rank candidates, run initial interviews, and match talent in-house without paying external recruiters. Bloomberg/Los Angeles Times coverage in February 2026 described AI as a staffing-industry threat, noting that automation could reduce the need for firms such as Hays and that disruption from automation has permanently removed some volumes. Jefferies rated Hays underperform, citing its exposure to professional white-collar recruitment markets, while BNP Paribas Exane cut staffing peers on technology risk and high automation exposure among temporary workers. Hays's own 2026 salary guide says more than 50% of surveyed organizations already use AI tools, 5% of roles are being fully replaced by AI, and 42% of organizations are upskilling existing employees rather than hiring new talent. That shift compresses both placement volumes and pricing power for recruiters. FY2025 net fees fell 11%, and the company cut consultant headcount as permanent recruitment weakness persisted. Stock has fallen about 70% from 118.10p on Nov 29, 2022 to roughly 35.08p, with no reverse split distortion in the ChatGPT-era price history.

name: S4 Capital plc
description: UK-listed digital advertising and marketing services group founded by Martin Sorrell, operating the Monks brand across creative content, media, data, technology services, and digital transformation. S4 positions itself as a digital-only, data-driven agency for global enterprise clients, with 6,345 people in 33 countries at the end of 2025. Reported 2025 revenue was GBP 754.8M, down 11.0%, and net revenue was GBP 673.0M, down 10.8%.
disruption: Generative and agentic AI are compressing the digital agency model from both sides: clients can automate strategy, creative production, media workflows, and marketing operations internally, while S4's technology-heavy clients are redirecting budgets toward AI infrastructure instead of agency services. S4's 2025 annual report says net revenue fell 10.8% and specifically points to shifting technology priorities toward AI capex rather than marketing. MediaPost reported that S4 blamed client caution, especially among tech clients focused more on AI developments and less on marketing budgets, while Q1 2026 net revenue fell another 8.9% reported and 5.0% like-for-like. Sorrell himself described agentic AI as fundamentally challenging the agency model, saying strategic and creative work is under attack and predicting fewer people as automation accelerates. S4 cut staff by 11.5% in 2025 and is trying to pivot through Monks.Flow and AI-enabled workflows, but the pivot underscores the disruption: AI lets clients and platforms produce more marketing output with fewer agency labor hours. Stock has fallen about 77% from 192.20p on Nov 29, 2022 to roughly 44.75p, with no ChatGPT-era split distortion.

name: PageGroup plc
description: UK-headquartered specialist recruitment consultancy operating the Page Executive, Michael Page, and Page Personnel brands across permanent, temporary, and contract hiring. The group places candidates in professional disciplines including accounting, finance, technology, engineering, sales, marketing, legal, and office support across 30+ countries. 2025 revenue fell 8.2% to GBP 1.60B, gross profit fell 13.3% to GBP 759.0M, and profit before tax fell 67.8% to GBP 16.2M as the global recruitment downturn persisted. Q1 2026 gross profit fell another 4.9% in constant currency, with the UK down 11.4% and EMEA down 9.2%.
disruption: Generative AI directly threatens PageGroup's recruiter-led placement model by automating candidate sourcing, resume screening, ranking, matching, and preliminary interviews, allowing employers and AI-native platforms to bring more of the hiring workflow in-house while paying lower fees for the parts still outsourced. Bloomberg/Los Angeles Times coverage in February 2026 described AI as a staffing-industry threat because employers can increasingly screen resumes, rank applicants, and run preliminary interviews without external recruiters; BNP Paribas Exane cut PageGroup to underperform on automation risk and high exposure of temporary workers to roles at risk of automation. The Financial Times argued that UK recruiters including PageGroup face weak hiring now and AI on the other side of the cycle, with AI agents able to scour vacancies and LinkedIn profiles and disintermediate recruitment firms; it specifically described PageGroup as more exposed than Robert Walters because of its lower-tier administrative placements. PageGroup's own Q1 2026 update shows the business pressure in the exposed regions: EMEA gross profit fell 9.2%, UK gross profit fell 11.4%, permanent recruitment fell 14% in the UK, and management reduced fee-earner headcount in EMEA and the UK while noting delayed hiring decisions and weak client/candidate confidence. Stock has fallen about 72% from 480.60p on Nov 29, 2022 to roughly 134.00p, with no ChatGPT-era split distortion in yfinance.

name: ZoomInfo Technologies Inc
description: Go-to-market intelligence platform providing B2B company data, contact data, buyer-intent signals, lead scoring, workflow tools, and sales/marketing automation for revenue teams. Formerly traded as ZI and changed its Nasdaq ticker to GTM in May 2025. Serves sales, marketing, operations, recruiting, and customer-success teams with more than 100 million company records, 500 million contact records, and billions of market signals. Q1 2026 revenue was $310.2M, up only 1.5% YoY, with 1,900 customers generating at least $100K in annual contract value and net revenue retention of 90%.
disruption: AI is undermining ZoomInfo's legacy sales-intelligence moat. Generative and agentic AI tools can now research accounts, enrich contacts, draft outreach, score leads, and orchestrate sales workflows directly inside CRMs and productivity suites, reducing willingness to pay premium subscription prices for standalone B2B data and prospecting software. After Q1 2026, ZoomInfo cut full-year revenue guidance from $1.247-1.267B to $1.185-1.205B and lowered free-cash-flow guidance; Barron's framed the resulting 32-35% one-day stock plunge as AI claiming another market-intelligence victim. Analysts at BTIG, Canaccord, Piper Sandler, and Stifel downgraded the stock, citing competitive pressure, AI-related disruption risk, elongated sales cycles, and purchasing pauses from AI and agentic confusion in the software vertical. The company is pivoting to AI-powered GTM workflows and more flexible hybrid/consumption pricing, but that pivot underscores the pressure: as AI makes account research and outreach automation cheaper and more embedded, customers renew at lower price points and the old seat/subscription model loses pricing power. Stock has fallen about 84% from $26.53 on Nov 29, 2022 to roughly $4.14, with no split distortion in yfinance under the active GTM ticker.

name: Wix.com Ltd
description: Cloud-based website creation and business-management platform for small businesses, creators, agencies, freelancers, and enterprises. Wix offers drag-and-drop website building, Wix Studio for professional designers, commerce, payments, scheduling, marketing, CRM, hosting, domains, and AI-powered creation tools. The company strengthened its AI push with the 2025 Base44 acquisition and the 2026 launch of Wix Harmony, its AI website-building system powered by a proprietary Wix LLM.
disruption: AI is attacking Wix's traditional website-builder value proposition from two sides. Consumer-friendly AI coding and design tools can now generate websites and lightweight apps from natural-language prompts, lowering switching costs and making customers question what they should pay for conventional drag-and-drop builders and professional web-design workflows. The pressure became explicit after Wix's Q1 2026 report: the stock fell about 27% in one day, RBC downgraded Wix to Sector Perform and cut its price target from $90 to $60, citing competitive concerns from AI consumer-level coding tools, with Partner weakness described as the clearest signal of coding headwinds affecting the business. Wix is trying to pivot into the disruption through Base44 and Harmony, but that pivot also creates margin pressure and investor uncertainty about whether the AI products can offset erosion in the legacy core. Q1 2026 revenue still grew 14% and bookings grew 15%, so this is not yet a revenue-collapse story; it is a market repricing of a website-builder incumbent whose core workflow is being commoditized by AI/vibe-coding alternatives. Stock has fallen about 36% from $84.55 on Nov 29, 2022 to roughly $54.47, with no split distortion in yfinance.

name: Straker Limited
description: New Zealand-headquartered AI language technology and translation services provider listed on the ASX. Straker provides language services, localization workflows, Verify, SwiftBridge AI, translation APIs, Slack and Microsoft Teams translation tools, and small-language-model products for enterprise and government clients. The company operates across APAC, Europe, the Middle East, Africa, and North America, with about 150 employees and FY2025 revenue of NZD 44.9M, down from NZD 50.0M in FY2024.
disruption: Generative AI and machine translation are structurally compressing the traditional human translation and localization services that historically drove Straker's revenue. Straker's own FY2025 annual report says traditional legacy translation services are declining and that Language Services revenue fell 24%, with legacy Language Services falling from 81% of revenue in FY2024 to 68% in FY2025. FY2026 H1 revenue fell another 15.3% to NZD 19.3M, with management citing reduced language-services project volume, pricing pressure, efficiency gains shared with clients, and customer attrition in the traditional enterprise TMS business. The company is pivoting hard toward AI-first products such as Verify, SwiftBridge AI, Tiri small language models, and token-based AI billing with IBM, which has protected gross margin and adjusted EBITDA but also confirms that the old per-project human translation model is being replaced by lower-cost AI workflows. Production headcount has fallen by more than 50% over two years, and H1 FY2026 headcount was down about 11% year over year as AI automation reduced the labor needed to deliver translation output. Stock has fallen about 80% from A$1.27 on Nov 29, 2022 to roughly A$0.255, with no split distortion in yfinance.

name: TeamViewer SE
description: German remote-access and digital workplace software provider best known for TeamViewer remote connectivity and support tools. The company serves more than 635,000 customers across IT support, endpoint management, digital employee experience, augmented-reality frontline work, and industrial operations. TeamViewer has been expanding beyond reactive remote support into TeamViewer ONE, Digital Employee Experience, and autonomous IT after acquiring 1E in 2025.
disruption: Generative AI and agentic IT support tools threaten the remote-support workflow that historically made TeamViewer valuable. AI assistants can diagnose issues, guide users through fixes, generate support documentation, and increasingly resolve standard endpoint problems automatically, reducing ticket volumes and the need for a human technician to open a remote-control session. Bernstein cut estimates in February 2026, warning that AI agents and LLM-powered support tools can replicate a significant portion of the value that sits on top of remote connectivity, while TeamViewer's FY2026 constant-currency revenue guidance of 0-3% implied continued Enterprise and SMB pressure.
